title: Japanese Chicken Teriyaki

subs.name: firm tofu (pressed, cut into thick slabs) for a vegan version — reduce cook time by 2 minutes per side
2 tbsp coconut aminos + 1 tsp salt for a soy-free glaze

ingredients.name: sugar (or mirin if available)
visual_checks: mirin gives a more authentic gloss; if using, reduce soy to 3 tbsp
ingredients.notes: mirin gives a more authentic gloss; if using, reduce soy to 3 tbsp

subs.name: maple syrup or date syrup
subs.notes: same quantity, slightly different flavour profile

subs.name: fresh lemon juice
subs.notes: use the same amount; adds brightness

steps.text: Stir together soy sauce, sugar, honey, rice vinegar and ginger-garlic paste in a bowl to make the teriyaki glaze. Taste — it should be salty, sweet and slightly tangy. Set aside two tablespoons of the glaze separately for basting later.

Pat the chicken dry, score the thicker pieces lightly with a knife, and season both sides with a small pinch of salt. Pour the remaining glaze over the chicken and toss to coat. Leave to marinate for 10 minutes at room temperature.

Heat oil in a heavy-bottomed pan or tawa over medium-high flame. Lift the chicken out of the marinade, letting excess drip off, and lay it flat in the pan. Cook without moving for 5–6 minutes until the underside is deep golden brown and releases cleanly.

Flip the chicken and cook the second side for 4–5 minutes. Pierce the thickest part with a skewer — the juices should run clear and the internal texture should feel firm with a little give. If the pan gets dry before the chicken is cooked through, splash in 2 tablespoons of water.

Pour the reserved glaze over the chicken and let it bubble for 1–2 minutes, tilting and spooning the sauce over the pieces as it reduces to a sticky, lacquered coating. Watch carefully at this stage — the sugar can burn in seconds.
Rest the chicken for 3 minutes off the heat before slicing diagonally into thick pieces. Arrange over steamed rice and spoon any remaining pan sauce on top. Finish with sesame seeds and spring onion.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Verify poultry doneness with a food thermometer where possible; do not rely on colour alone.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: chicken → firm tofu (pressed, cut into thick slabs) for a vegan version — reduce cook time by 2 minutes per side; soy-sauce → 2 tbsp coconut aminos + 1 tsp salt for a soy-free glaze (less savoury depth but tolerable); honey → maple syrup or date syrup (same quantity, slightly different flavour profile); vinegar → fresh lemon juice (use the same amount; adds brightness)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: A thick tawa or cast-iron kadai works perfectly in place of a Western skillet. Soy sauce (shoyu) is available at most large Indian grocery stores and online. Mirin can be replaced by dissolving 2 tsp sugar in 1 tbsp water with a splash of vinegar (sirka). Serve over steamed sona masoori or ponni rice for a satisfying meal.
